title: St Benedict's Church, Haltham-on-Bain

infobox.value: [Greenstone](/wiki/Greensand "Greensand") with [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") dressings and some red brick. Roofs [slated](/wiki/Slate "Slate")

is a [redundant](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church") [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") church in the village of [Haltham-on-Bain](/wiki/Haltham "Haltham"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"), England. It is recorded in the [National Heritage List for England](/wiki/National_Heritage_List_for_England "National Heritage List for England") as a designated Grade I [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building#England_and_Wales "Listed building"),[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) and is under the care of the [Churches Conservation Trust](/wiki/Churches_Conservation_Trust "Churches Conservation Trust").[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2) It stands between the [River Bain](/wiki/River_Bain "River Bain") and the [A153 road](/wiki/A153_road "A153 road") connecting [Horncastle](/wiki/Horncastle,_Lincolnshire "Horncastle, Lincolnshire") with [Coningsby](/wiki/Coningsby "Coningsby").[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2)

Historic England, ["Churchyard cross to Church of St Benedict, Haltham (1215290)"](https://HistoricEngland.org.uk/listing/the-list/list-entry/1215290?section=official-list-entry), *National Heritage List for England*, retrieved 15 May 2011

**St Benedict's Church** is a [redundant](/wiki/Redundant_church "Redundant church") [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") church in the village of [Haltham-on-Bain](/wiki/Haltham "Haltham"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"), England. It is recorded in the [National Heritage List for England](/wiki/National_Heritage_List_for_England "National Heritage List for England") as a designated Grade I [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building#England_and_Wales "Listed building"),[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) and is under the care of the [Churches Conservation Trust](/wiki/Churches_Conservation_Trust "Churches Conservation Trust").[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2) It stands between the [River Bain](/wiki/River_Bain "River Bain") and the [A153 road](/wiki/A153_road "A153 road") connecting [Horncastle](/wiki/Horncastle,_Lincolnshire "Horncastle, Lincolnshire") with [Coningsby](/wiki/Coningsby "Coningsby").[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The church dates from the early 12th century. Alterations and additions were made later during that century, in the late 13th century, and in the 14th and the 15th centuries.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) In 1842 it was described as "an ancient structure, which has suffered much from the hand of time...".[[3]](#cite_note-genuki-3) The church was [restored](/wiki/Victorian_restoration "Victorian restoration") in 1880 and again in 1891, the latter restoration increasing the seating from 67 to 140. It was declared redundant in October 1977.[[3]](#cite_note-genuki-3)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![A plain wooden door over which is a round arch with a carved tympanum](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Interior_of_St_Benedict%2C_Haltham-on-Bain_-_geograph.org.uk_-_450903.jpg/330px-Interior_of_St_Benedict%2C_Haltham-on-Bain_-_geograph.org.uk_-_450903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_St_Benedict,_Haltham-on-Bain_-_geograph.org.uk_-_450903.jpg)

[Norman](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") doorway, with an ornate [tympanum](/wiki/Tympanum_(architecture) "Tympanum (architecture)") above

### Exterior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Exterior")]

St Benedict's is constructed in [greenstone](/wiki/Greensand "Greensand") [rubble](/wiki/Rubble "Rubble") with [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") [ashlar](/wiki/Ashlar "Ashlar") dressings. The [gables](/wiki/Gable "Gable") are in red brick, and red brick has been used in places for patching. The roofs are [slated](/wiki/Slate "Slate"), and the [bellcote](/wiki/Bellcote "Bellcote") is [timber boarded](/wiki/Weatherboarding "Weatherboarding"). Its plan consists of a [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") with a north [aisle](/wiki/Aisle "Aisle") and a south porch, and a [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel"). The bellcote sits towards the west end of the nave roof. At the west end of the church is a 14th-century doorway above which is a three-light window dating from the 15th century. In the west gable are two re-set [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") stones carved with heads. The north aisle has a two-light window in the north wall, a blocked doorway, a two-light and a three-light window in the north wall, and a three-light window in the east wall. The north wall of the chancel contains a two-light and a three-light window and two blocked [ogee](/wiki/Ogee "Ogee")-headed windows.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) The east window has four lights and contains [Decorated](/wiki/Decorated_Gothic "Decorated Gothic") [tracery](/wiki/Tracery "Tracery").[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2) In the south wall of the chancel are a two-light and a three-light window, two ogee-headed windows, and an ogee-headed doorway. The south wall of the nave contains a two-light 13th-century window with Y-tracery, The gabled porch dates from the 14th century. It has an arched doorway, stepped angle [buttresses](/wiki/Buttress "Buttress") with [crocketted](/wiki/Crocket "Crocket") [pinnacles](/wiki/Pinnacle "Pinnacle") and a cross [finial](/wiki/Finial "Finial").[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) The inner doorway is [Norman](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") in style, with a round head, three orders of roll [moulding](/wiki/Molding_(decorative) "Molding (decorative)"), and a carved [tympanum](/wiki/Tympanum_(architecture) "Tympanum (architecture)") containing a [Maltese cross](/wiki/Maltese_cross "Maltese cross") in a circle, a fan-shaped shell to its left, and a knot in a circle to the right.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2)

### Interior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Interior")]

The [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_(architecture) "Arcade (architecture)") between the nave and the north aisle has three [bays](/wiki/Bay_(architecture) "Bay (architecture)") with octagonal [piers](/wiki/Pier_(architecture) "Pier (architecture)"). The two bays to the east have late-12th-century rounded arches, and the west bay has a pointed arch, probably dating from the 13th century. The chancel arch is pointed and dates from the 19th century. In the chancel are a 14th-century [piscina](/wiki/Piscina "Piscina") with two ogee arches, four brackets for statues, and an [aumbry](/wiki/Aumbry "Aumbry"). The [pulpit](/wiki/Pulpit "Pulpit") dates from the 18th century; above it is a [tester](/wiki/Sounding_board "Sounding board"), and below it are seats in boxes.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1) Arranged around the pulpit are pews dating from various periods facing in different directions. Some of these are family pews which have been reconstructed using wood from a 15th-century screen.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cct-2) The octagonal [font](/wiki/Baptismal_font "Baptismal font") dates from the 14th century, its bowl being carved with [fleurons](/wiki/Fleuron_(architectural) "Fleuron (architectural)"). Also inside the church are boards painted with the [royal arms](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom") of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") and biblical texts.[[1]](#cite_note-hg-1)

## External features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: External features")]

In the churchyard is a 14th-century cross consisting of an octagonal shaft on a square base. It is designated as a Grade II listed building.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Haltham-on-Bain&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]
